questions.text: Where was Lencho's house located?
options: On the crest of a low hill
questions.explanation: Lencho's house was the only one in the entire valley, situated on the top of a low hill.

questions.text: What was the only thing that the earth needed, according to Lencho?
options: A downpour or at least a shower
questions.explanation: Lencho was a farmer who was waiting for rain to ensure a good harvest of his corn.

questions.text: What did Lencho compare the large raindrops to?
questions.explanation: He compared them to new coins—the big drops being ten-cent pieces and the small ones fives.

questions.text: What destroyed Lencho's fields?
questions.explanation: The rain suddenly changed into a hailstorm with very large hailstones that lasted for an hour.

questions.text: The hailstones were compared to ______ in the text.
questions.explanation: The author describes the hailstones as truly resembling new silver coins or 'frozen pearls'.

questions.text: Lencho said, 'A plague of ______ would have left more than this.'
questions.explanation: He used the comparison of a locust attack to express the total devastation caused by the hail.

questions.text: What was Lencho's only hope after the destruction of his crops?
questions.explanation: Lencho had deep, unwavering faith in God and believed God would see everything and help him.

questions.text: How much money did Lencho ask for in his first letter?
questions.explanation: He asked for 100 pesos to sow his field again and to live until the next crop came.

questions.text: Who was the first person to see Lencho's letter at the post office?
questions.explanation: A postman, who was also an employee at the office, found the letter addressed to God and laughed.

questions.text: What was the initial reaction of the postmaster when he saw the letter?
questions.explanation: Like the postman, he first laughed at the address but soon became serious about the sender's faith.

questions.text: What kind of a person was the postmaster?
questions.explanation: The text describes the postmaster as a fat, amiable (friendly and pleasant) fellow.

questions.text: Why did the postmaster decide to answer the letter?
options: So as not to shake the writer's faith in God
questions.explanation: He was impressed by Lencho's deep faith and didn't want him to lose it.

questions.text: How much money was the postmaster able to collect?
questions.explanation: Despite his best efforts and contributing part of his salary, he could only collect a little more than half—70 pesos.

questions.text: How did the postmaster sign the letter back to Lencho?
questions.explanation: He signed it 'God' so that Lencho would believe the money actually came from God.

questions.text: Why was Lencho not surprised when he found the money in the envelope?
options: His confidence in God was absolute
questions.explanation: His faith was so strong that he expected a reply and help from God as a matter of fact.

questions.text: Why did Lencho become angry after counting the money?
options: The money was less than he asked for
questions.explanation: He found only 70 pesos instead of the 100 he had requested.

questions.text: Whom did Lencho blame for the missing money?
questions.explanation: He believed God could not have made a mistake and concluded the employees took the rest.

questions.text: What did Lencho call the post-office employees?
questions.explanation: He referred to them as a 'bunch of crooks' in his second letter to God.

questions.text: What was the irony in the story?
options: The people who helped Lencho were called crooks by him
questions.explanation: Irony is when the opposite of what is expected happens; here, the benefactors were accused of being thieves.

questions.text: How did Lencho fix the stamp to the envelope?
options: With a blow of his fist
questions.explanation: He licked the stamp and then affixed it to the envelope with a strong blow of his fist.

questions.text: What did Lencho do for a living?
options: He was an ox of a man (farmer)
questions.explanation: Lencho was a hardworking farmer who worked like an animal in his fields.

questions.text: What was the color of the field after the hailstorm?
options: White as if covered with salt
questions.explanation: The hail covered everything in white, making the field look like it was covered in salt.

questions.text: Lencho's letter reached God through:
questions.explanation: In the story, he literally posted a letter addressed to 'God' at the local post-office.

questions.text: The field of ripe corn was dotted with ______.
questions.explanation: The flowers always promised a good harvest.

questions.text: How did Lencho feel when it started raining?
questions.explanation: He went out to have the pleasure of feeling the rain on his body.

questions.text: What did Lencho think of the post office employees?
questions.explanation: He believed they stole 30 pesos from the envelope God sent.

questions.text: What was the weather like when Lencho and his family were having dinner?
questions.explanation: Huge mountains of clouds were seen approaching from the northeast.

questions.text: What was the name of the author of 'A Letter to God'?
questions.explanation: Gregorio Lopez Fuentes is the Mexican writer of this story.

questions.text: In his second letter, Lencho asked God not to send the money through the mail because:
questions.explanation: He suspected the staff of stealing his money.

questions.text: What does 'ox of a man' mean?
questions.explanation: It is a metaphor used to describe his physical strength and capacity for hard work.

questions.text: The postmaster decided to help Lencho by ______.
options: Collecting money from employees and friends
questions.explanation: He collected money as an 'act of charity'.

questions.text: For how long did the hail rain on the valley?
questions.explanation: The hail rained for an hour on the house, the garden, the hillside, and the whole valley.

questions.text: What was Lencho's soul filled with when the storm passed?
questions.explanation: Looking at the total destruction, he was deeply saddened.

questions.text: Lencho had written the letter on a ______.
questions.explanation: At daybreak on Sunday, he began to write the letter.

questions.text: The postmaster was impressed by Lencho's ______.
questions.explanation: He exclaimed, 'What faith! I wish I had the faith of the man who wrote this letter.'

questions.text: How many letters did Lencho write to God in total?
questions.explanation: He wrote the first letter asking for money, and a second letter after receiving only 70 pesos.

questions.text: What does the story 'A Letter to God' teach us?
questions.explanation: It explores the power of innocent faith and the kindness of strangers.

questions.text: Where did Lencho go to post his letter?
questions.explanation: He went to town to place the letter in the mail.

questions.text: The corn field belonged to ______.
questions.explanation: It was Lencho's personal property and source of income.

questions.text: Which of these words describes Lencho's character?
questions.explanation: He is depicted as simple, hardworking, and having child-like faith.
